author: @roidroid
text:
So it seems you could start with a long strip of cardboard, coated in conductive ink on both sides so it's a capacitor, but fold it up in a zig-zag manner.
It'd work as one big capacitor _(or many in parallel, if you wish to think of it like that)_.  Assuming the ink can stretch that far around the corners _(depends on the thickness of the cardboard i guess)_ without breaking.  You may also need a layer of plastic between each fold so each layer doesn't touch the next, assuming that's nessesary.
You'd end up with a capacitive cardboard cube.

author: @IAMainvision
text: Yet another very informative video! You may also want to try 'my method' of using a circuit drawn on acetate (overhead projector transparency) by a laser printer. The carbon print is conductive too. You can electroplate it in Copper Sulphate for better conductivity and it produces a printed schematic you can roll up with your components soldered in (or conductive glue) to use when space is an issue.

author: @sceptic33
text: which one, the concertina coil or the copper/iron hybrid?  I was just looking back through my old doodles, the best one was actually just a fat sinwave folded at all the zero points...  with another sheet of paper on top to stop any shorts...  could make a nice cheap transformer... my dad reckoned I should patent the idea but what the hey... it´s out there now in the public domain and someone else has probably done it before anyway...

author: @sceptic33
text: you can do a nice coil by drawing a zigzag with curly straight bits that don´t cross when you then fold the paper into a concertina type thing...  john bedini said somewhere that a coil made from iron wire will produce a magnetic field opposite to that made by a copper wire if both are energized with the same current... what if the "wire" is made of iron and copper paticles? 0 field? could be interesting...

text: Lol. Yes it is and I am working on improving that.

author: @picnet
text: Very educational and nice series of videos Rob, 40 Ohms / cm is significantly better than bareconductive's ink.

author: @MathewTitus
